topics.tldr: This week's discussion on AI crawling and publisher controls highlights new tools for managing AI bot access and monetization, ongoing legal battles over scraped content and copyright, and academic proposals for regulatory frameworks including licensing and transparency.

items.title: AI llms-full.txt
items.frame: Cloudflare's AI Crawl Control offers publishers tools to monitor, manage, and monetize AI crawler access to their content, including a 'pay per crawl' option.
items.quote: AI Crawl Control (formerly AI Audit) gives you visibility into which AI services are accessing your content, and provides tools to manage access according to your preferences.

items.title: Hey Orwell: thousands of AI‑written, edited or 'polished' books are being sold
items.frame: This piece discusses recent lawsuits against AI companies (Anthropic, Grammarly) for copyright infringement and identity misappropriation, highlighting legal challenges of AI-generated content.
items.quote: In 2025, the artificial intelligence company Anthropic, best known for creating the chatbot Claude, agreed to pay up to US$1.5 billion to thousands of authors after a judge ruled that the company had infringed upon their copyrights.

items.title: Danny Friedmann, 'Signature-style' | Private Law Theory
items.frame: This academic analysis proposes a regulatory framework for AI training on copyrighted works, advocating for opt-in or statutory licensing and transparency to address 'surrogate extraction.'
items.quote: Rather than recognizing a property right in style or prohibiting imitation, it proposes treating certain forms of AI training as a regulated use, supported by opt-in or statutory licensing mechanisms, transparency obligations, and tiered remedies.

topics.tldr: This week's reporting reveals a critical and widespread crisis in AI agent security and governance, with multiple studies indicating that a vast majority of enterprises are experiencing security incidents, scope violations, and significant gaps in their ability to monitor, control, and audit autonomous agents, despite high executive confidence. Organizations are struggling to build adequate infrastructure and governance frameworks to manage the rapid deployment and inherent risks of AI agents, leading to calls for dedicated security investments and evolved strategies.

items.title: AI Agent Security 2026: What 88% of Companies Got Wrong
items.frame: Multiple studies reveal 88% of organizations experienced AI agent security incidents, citing over-permissioning, weak authentication, and governance gaps.
items.quote: Gravitee surveyed over 900 executives and technical practitioners and found that 88% of organizations reported confirmed or suspected AI agent security incidents in the past year.

items.title: Most enterprises can't stop stage-three AI agent threats, VentureBeat survey finds
items.frame: A VentureBeat survey reveals most enterprises lack the security architecture to stop advanced AI agent threats, with a significant gap between monitoring and isolation.
items.quote: A VentureBeat three-wave survey of 108 qualified enterprises found that the gap is not an edge case. It is the most common security architecture in production today.

items.title: AI Agent Infrastructure: The Foundation You Need to Deploy Intelligent Agents at Scale
items.frame: This piece argues that robust AI agent infrastructure, encompassing orchestration, monitoring, and security, is crucial for scalable and reliable agent deployment.
items.quote: AI agent infrastructure is the entire technical foundation that allows your autonomous agents to function, communicate, make decisions, and perform actions reliably in production environments.

items.title: Everyone is Deploying AI Agents. Who is Governing Them?
items.frame: Enterprises are rapidly deploying AI agents without adequate governance, leading to widespread security incidents and a crisis in oversight.
items.quote: Only 14% agents require human approval before deployment, 88% companies report agent security incidents, and just 6% of security budgets address the risk.

items.title: AI Agent Security Risks Are Already Here, According to Research
items.frame: A survey reveals widespread enterprise AI agent adoption, including shadow AI, leading to frequent scope violations and a significant gap in security and governance.
items.quote: AI agents are already embedded in core workflows, already exceeding intended permissions on a regular basis, and the governance and detection mechanisms needed to manage them are still catching up.

items.title: Enterprise AI Agent Security Survey Report
items.frame: This survey report details the widespread, decentralized adoption of AI agents in enterprises, highlighting routine scope violations, security incidents, and governance gaps.
items.quote: Nearly half (47%) of respondents experienced a security incident involving an AI agent in the past year.

items.title: More Than Half of Organizations Experience AI Agent Scope Violations, Cloud Security Alliance Study Finds
items.frame: A Cloud Security Alliance study reveals over half of organizations experience AI agent scope violations and nearly half have had security incidents due to inadequate governance.
items.quote: Commissioned by Zenity, the leading security and governance platform for AI, the Enterprise AI Security Starts with AI Agents survey found that 53% of organizations have had AI agents exceed their intended permissions, leaving them vulnerable to increased risk.

topics.tldr: This week saw continued legal battles over AI training data copyright, with new lawsuits against major AI companies and ongoing discussions about fair use. Regulatory bodies in the US, EU, and UK are actively shaping policy, exploring licensing frameworks, and addressing transparency and accountability in AI development.

items.title: AI Legal Watch: April
items.frame: The White House released a National Policy Framework for AI, recommending Congress consider licensing frameworks for copyrighted material and federal protections for digital replicas.
items.quote: The emphasize revolves around the need to protect American creators, publishers, and innovators from AI-generated outputs that infringe their protected content, while simultaneously preserving lawful innovation and free expression.

items.title: Can AI training result in copyright infringement? The state of play in the UK, EU and China
items.frame: Analyzes the differing legal interpretations and ongoing cases regarding AI training and copyright infringement across the UK, EU, and China.
items.quote: This issue remains unresolved and hotly contested in most worldwide jurisdictions, not just the UK, EU and China.

items.title: Apple's AI Lawsuit: Scraped Data, Shattered Trust & Future
items.frame: Reports on a proposed class-action lawsuit against Apple for allegedly scraping millions of YouTube videos without consent to train its AI systems.
items.quote: In April 2026, a lawsuit alleging that Apple scraped millions of YouTube videos without permission sent shockwaves across the tech and creator ecosystems.

items.title: The White House's National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence: what it means and what comes next
items.frame: Summarizes the White House's legislative recommendations for a national AI governance approach, including federal preemption of state laws and protections for digital replicas.
items.quote: The Framework recommends that Congress provide protections for individuals affected by the unauthorized distribution or commercial use of AI-generated digital replicas of their voice, likeness, or other identifiable attributes, while exempting parody, satire, news reporting, and other expressive works protected by the First Amendment.

items.title: Generative AI – IP cases and policy tracker
items.frame: A tracker detailing various ongoing intellectual property cases related to generative AI across different jurisdictions, including new complaints against AI companies.
items.quote: In this tracker, we provide an insight on the various intellectual property cases relating to generative AI going through the courts, as well as anticipated policy and legislative developments.

items.title: AI Round-up - April 2026
items.frame: Discusses regulatory recalibration in the EU and UK, focusing on copyright, transparency, and the UK creative sector's move towards a licensing-first approach for AI training data.
items.quote: March delivered a decisive shift towards agentic AI at scale, a regulatory recalibration across the EU and UK, and a sharpened focus on copyright, provenance, and platform accountability.

items.title: Whose AI Is It Anyway? Key Developments in the Evolving Relationship Between AI and IP
items.frame: Highlights the divergence across major jurisdictions on key AI intellectual property issues, such as the use of copyrighted data for AI training and the copyrightability of AI outputs.
items.quote: Divergence across major jurisdictions on key issues such as the protectability of AI tools and outputs, the use of copyrighted data for AI training, and the requirements for inventorship and authorship require companies to adopt different IP strategies in different countries.

items.title: Copyright owners v AI developers: the emerging trends
items.frame: Examines the evolving relationship between copyright owners and AI developers, noting recent letters of claim from publishers and the UK government's abandoned TDM exception proposal.
items.quote: In February 2026, a group of almost 40 independent publishers, coordinated by the Independent Publishers Guild (IPG), sent letters of claim to several leading AI developers.

items.title: AI Quarterly | A Review of AI Law, Policy & Practice | April 2026
items.frame: Reviews the Trump Administration's AI Framework and Senator Blackburn's proposed framework, noting differences in copyright protections and liability for AI developers.
items.quote: Blackburn's draft generally reflects the priorities outlined in the AI Framework, with notable differences in the areas of copyright protections, liability for AI developers, and the proposed repeal of Section 230 of the Communications Act.

items.title: Regulatory update Data protection, AI, IT and IP
items.frame: Reports on a Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) clarification regarding the scope of the 'pastiche' exception in copyright law, relevant to 'sampling'.
items.quote: 14 April 2026 – The Court of Justice of the European Union clarifies the scope of the exception relating to pastiche in the context of "sampling".

topics.tldr: This week's analysis highlights Google's new AI Mode in Chrome, which embeds publisher content directly, raising concerns about traffic and control. A Canadian committee has also recommended mandatory labeling for AI-generated content and opt-in consent for using copyrighted works in AI training. Additionally, a report details significant organic search traffic losses for various platforms due to AI search engines answering queries directly.

items.title: Google's AI Mode in Chrome is doing what iframes couldn't
items.frame: Google's new AI Mode in Chrome embeds publisher sites directly within its interface, potentially reducing outbound clicks and overriding publisher controls.
items.quote: "Google's AI Mode in Chrome embeds publisher websites inside its own interface while ignoring X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN and content-security-policy: frame-ancestors 'self' headers - the same security directives that Google uses to prevent embedding of its own properties, and that Chrome itself is designed to enforce."

items.title: Should all artificial intelligence-generated content be clearly labelled as such?
items.frame: A Canadian House of Commons committee recommends mandatory labeling for AI-generated content and opt-in consent for using copyrighted works in AI training.
items.quote: "It also called on the government to 'establish a clear opt-in consent requirement for the use of copyrighted works in the training of artificial intelligence systems, ensuring that creators' works may not be used for text and data mining or model development without their prior authorization.'"

items.title: Platforms Losing Visibility Due to AI: The Data, the Diagnosis, and the Recovery Playbook
items.frame: A report details significant organic search traffic losses for various platforms due to AI search engines answering queries directly, and offers strategies for recovery.
items.quote: "Platforms across every industry are losing organic search visibility not because their content got worse, but because AI search engines are answering user queries directly without sending traffic to the source."
